Welcome everyone to Total Breakthrough. Today we're going to be talking about the Rodriguez Intervention Series. You know, so let's get started. I want to, I think what we'll do is we'll start with, I want to present a concept which has been really helpful to me in understanding this and also most of the interventions. And then we'll discuss some of the strategies that were used in this intervention. Then we can go ahead and open up the line and talk about, kind of open it up. So with John Rodriguez, you know, he came into the, he, Tony picked him because his sons had basically applied John to get him in a position. John was, in his description, was mourning for his wife who was tragically killed and had basically, you know, so many parts of his life had fallen, fallen apart that he was, that he had lost their home and they were living in public housing and the boys were really worried about him. And so I want to talk today about how do you help a client who has really, I guess, Tony, the way that Tony looks at the John Rodriguez case is really about someone who's let go of the pride, their self-esteem, and their standards for themselves. And this is maybe a more extreme case than any of the others that we've seen because in the others people were still living in their own homes and they still had some of the amenities that they had in most of their lives. In this case, they didn't. So a content that I'd like to share with you today is about short-term versus long-term reinforcement. And this is Tony talked about in his, in his director camera piece and his coaching piece that there is a, one of the goals for John was to help him to get out of, you know, you can put it in different ways, his story, his self-pity, his kind of falling apart in a pattern. Now, so there are two kinds of, when we talk about obviously a central concept for us is the meeting of needs, right? And we all meet needs, all of us have to meet needs every day. And now we have different timescales in which we meet needs. So we meet needs in the short-term and the medium-term and the long-term. So long-term reinforcement is when your needs are met by actions that are good for you in the long-term. It's according to a plan. And when you meet your needs according to a long-term, you're actually often sacrificing or delaying gratification in the short-term in order to get some greater gain in the long-term. Right? And so we often call this proactive or long-term reinforcement. And it's, you know, it's a key skill in moving forward in your life, building something that takes sacrifice or effort now. But it creates results that you can't get in the short-term if you just go for short-term reinforcement. The other kind of reinforcement we can talk about is short-term reinforcement, which is the opposite. It's basically that your needs are met now. And it doesn't matter whether it's bad for you or good for you long-term. We have ways of meeting our needs immediately. And very often, you know, people feel desperate. They feel hungry. They feel upset. And they can meet their needs and meet in the short-term. Very often when people talk about habits that they're struggling with or the patterns or emotions that they're struggling with, is that their system is set up so that they're meeting their needs. They basically have very few ways not to succumb to meeting their needs in the short-term. They're so hungry, so upset, so, you know, thrown off by some situation. And it meets your needs in the moment, but it hurts your health in the long-term. And this can extend to not only things that you do in the short-term to meet your needs, but also things that you avoid doing that would have been good for you, like exercise, financial planning, various kinds of discipline, and also your relationships, right? People can get upset with a partner or with someone in their family. And it's – or they get in a bad mood, and it's more satisfying to be in a bad mood or to get angry or to get in a fight rather than see things from another perspective, see things from the perspective of the people living with you, right? And so these short-term ways of meeting our needs, short-term reinforcements, are very powerful, and it's something that we share with the animal whole – animal kingdom. And it can become very addictive, and even if it's humiliating, even if it's against your identity, people, we can get stuck in that. And in fact, I would argue that probably most of us have at some point an area of our lives where we tend to give in to the short-term even though we're jeopardizing the long plan for ourselves. And so one of the challenges of coaching in any situation, not just in extreme case like this, is getting the person engaged in the kind of goals and aspirations that require long-term reinforcement. And when right now they're hooked on short-term reinforcement, how do you let – get someone to let go of what feels easy and comfortable in the moment and get them to hold out for the long-term rewards? Now, most cases people don't – people actually have the skills of having – they often have the skills of holding out for long-term rewards, but they've had something happen in their life that made them lose faith in the long plan for the life, right? So sometimes a person has a shock or a tragedy or an injustice, and they lose faith that if they continue life in a certain way or believe in the future or continue to give, then they believe that's no longer going to ever work to give them a happy life, and then they give up long-term rewards or short-term comforts. And they may drop their standards for themselves. Mark, could I add something here that I think is important for coaches to think about? Absolutely. Absolutely. This isn't the first time we mention it, but in this type of situation when there's a pattern that is very focused on short-term comfort, especially when it comes to depression or anger, overeating or any kind of habit that long-term has a negative effect, we always look at the secondary benefit the person or gain that the person is getting from allowing them to go to this lower level of emotion or to see what happens. And we think to what they consider their bottom. Absolutely. Yeah, I just wanted to add that in, that there's, that's always an important question that you ask yourself when you're talking to someone in this pattern. So what, what are they gaining by being here? What are they gaining by being stuck? What are they gaining by repeating this comfort-based behavior that will not give them what they ultimately want? Yeah, and that's really... A sort of light-hearted way to think about it that I think is helpful when talking to a client about it. I always like the example of people that are chronically late for something, because so many of us have been in that place before where we just keep showing up late for things. And so I always try to ask myself if I'm showing up late a lot or someone else I know, what's the secondary gain I get from being late? Do I start my interaction with the person getting their forgiveness for being late? Do I think that I'm busier because I'm late? And so that's, that's an example that we've all probably experienced at some point in our life, a pattern of being late. Absolutely. Which I don't think holds as much judgment as things like overeating or not exercising or something like that. And I think what's, what, and one thing that I totally agree with you, and I think it's also, there's a way that when, you know, the way I'm talking about the short-term and long-term, which is kind of like a quasi-scientific way of looking at things or objective, it may not be the best way to talk to people about their own patterns. So I was trying to bring a generalization because like you say, the secondary gain is really the most, and very often it's a social secondary gain. Very often if you are, if someone is, you know, if you're in a family group or a group of people who care for each other, the person who has let go and needs most, and is most helpless often gets a lot more control, gets a lot more sympathy from people around them because people will extend and give them, you know, sympathy and let them off the hook and make excuses for them, whatever. So that's an example of people in a, you know, social group giving secondary gain to each other. You know, that's another way of getting reinforced, right? Mm-hmm. And so, so Tony's, you know, what Tony wanted to get for John here is that there were some forces that would turn John around and get him back on track. And these were, and these were also the emotional forces that were strongest in John. And so, you know, these were like the respect of his kids, the self-respect of rising to a challenge and being seen as such by his children, accepting and appreciating the advantages that he has, succeeding as a father. These are things that Tony wanted to, at the very beginning of the intervention, help John to experience. And in that first intervention, where Tony's sitting around the round table with John, and he's really looking for some way that he can elevate John and give him a lot of reinforcement and respect. And so when he found out from John's story that he had quit drugs because he was concerned for his kids, Tony really wanted to find that. He gave him tremendous respect for that and gave him a kind of a new reference point for everything that he does in the intervention. So Tony talked about certain strategies that he used to disrupt this type of John's, the weak saw as John's focus. You know, John's pattern of being in the short-term comfort of just sleeping and eating and, you know, and people get very hurt. They will go into self-soothing, coping type of behaviors. And so John was one of, you know, an example of that. So the first one was the jackpot strategy. Now, the jackpot strategy was Tony basically said that people aren't. When it comes to helping people to, you know, training people or teaching people, people are a lot like dolphins in that you can't really, you can only get a dolphin's interest by giving them something that's really interesting to them. And one of the strategies that dolphin trainers use is they will give, it's called the jackpot strategy. So they will actually overwhelm a dolphin with a tremendous amount of fish to really get them fully engaged and attentive to the amount of reward that they got. And it's out of proportion with anything else that's happened. And it gets to kind of boost them out of that, of, you know, being in a stubborn relationship or resisting until that they get the jackpot and they, you know, they suddenly become very amenable that needs to be met and they're interested. So that was the one of the strategies that he gave John that he, by, you know, he, by taking him into this environment. And I think what's important is that he was looking for a way to get John out of that pattern of, I guess, I'll back up here. One thing that happens when people go get addicted to the short term reinforcement pattern where they're just soothing themselves and they don't have to be as responsible and people around them are kind of giving or making, you know, kind of letting them off the hook. Is that the jackpot pattern is a way of jolting someone out of that stubborn pattern where you're getting reinforced just basically by yourself or by giving to yourself and you're not, you know, you're not having to do very much for other people. The second strategy that he talked about was correcting the hierarchy. Correcting the hierarchy happens when a parent has, when the, basically the child has become, feels more responsible for the life situation and often survival than the parent, where they basically take the leadership role in the relationship. So in this case, John's older son was, had expressed more concern and worry and vision for the family than the father had. And this was a, so Tony helped them, so this is what we call correcting the hierarchy and this happens anytime this happens where you have a child who is substantially more attentive to the needs of the group than the parent and the child will often take on the disproportionate amount of responsibility. And the parent may also, in addition to, may also feel, you know, kind of humiliated and feel a sense of loss of pride and a loss of status in the family. And so one way that Tony wanted to correct that is by having them do this, this jump into the river where, where the sons would seek the comfort of the father, they'd seek his leadership in doing that. So that was a second strategy that they used. Yeah, I loved that. And also, if I could say something about the jump, that was also an ordeal, really. It was facing his fears. They all had to face their fears. And one of the larger issues, I think that John had was that by going towards comfort and losing all sense of responsibility after his wife died, he had basically become terrified of feeling loss or fear. So he kept stuffing himself with, with what he could, which was food to give himself love. And he really never went through a grieving process. So I see that part of grief is facing fear. And that jump was the beginning step, but then everything he did in his training and his ordeal with the military was also facing his fears physically and in terms of, you know, what he was able to do. And we saw it very clearly with the way he was in his physical body, but it was going on mentally as well. So that's a great, you know, that's a great point because when I was talking about when people lose faith in a long plan and they just go for the short term, what can also happen is when someone, he basically had this grief and he, you can fill up your grief with short term kind of self soothing coping, you know, or addictions. So he was not allowing himself to feel what you learned in grief. What you learn in grief is that, you know, life is fragile, that people need to be cherished, that there is uncertainty in the world, the fear is real, but you find a way to live with that reality. And by becoming hooked in this kind of self soothing pattern, he was not even allowing himself to experience that, you know, that reality of life and death. And it's also not uncommon, I think we've talked about this before, that if someone loses a very important person to them, they will sometimes put on as much weight as the person weighed. Yes, yes. I just find that fascinating. Yeah. So the third strategy was the ordeal, and this is where Tony signed, you know, where John signed up for the Marines for 10 days. And this is a kind of ordeal where, again, he was expected to join a group and they had certain standards and certain practices and he would, to the occasion, and be with them. It was really important to Tony that the sons saw John doing this and that they were inspired by him and that actually was very successful. The older son actually later on joined the Marines based on the experience of seeing his father join. And so we've kind of gone over the ordeal. The fourth part is a strategy called, well, basically I'm calling a reversal of privilege and we've seen this happen with the Smith, with the Smith family intervention. And so we're, again, Tony hooked, you know, connected John with a staff sergeant and the Marines and they were going to go for a run and the staff sergeant said, hold on, I'm talking about I need to change my legs. And he took his, and as he was an amputee and he put on his legs for running. And John felt, you know, basically put in his place what I'm calling it is a reversal of privilege. And I think what happens is when people feel hurt, they feel kind of socially justified in having other people take care of them. They feel like, well, I'm the victim here. I'm the one who needs help. People should be paying attention to me. Now, if you find someone who's dramatically more in need of help or has much more difficult situation in life than you do, then there's a tendency for people to feel a reversal of privilege, meaning I felt like I was a privileged person. I was a little disadvantaged person. I was the one who needed to be taken care of. But now I see this person has it much harder than me. They actually should have the privilege of having people take care of them. And Tony's used this from, you know, since then, September 11th. That was when he did that intervention. That was one of his primary strategies is when people were panicking in the room because of the terrorist attacks, yes, there's people in this room who have actually lost other people and they deserve our attention. They're the ones who are in more need than we are. And got the whole room of people to get a hold of themselves because they felt that reversal of privilege. They're not the ones who are privileged to feel sorry for themselves and let go of everything. They needed to step up and help the other person. So that was another strategy where, again, you know, John could, he had to internally, if he was planning to succumb to a short-term reinforcement in other words, comfort himself, make an excuse, say he can't do it. And then when he was running with a staff sergeant who had, you know, his prosthetic limbs, he could not take that position in good faith. And so the reversal of privilege helped him to temper himself and rise to the occasion. The other strategy was, of course, getting a job where Tony set him up with a job and Tony didn't just get him a job, he got him a job in the very best dream restaurant. And the way this works is that when you set someone up with a dream situation, and this is not something that's maybe accessible to us as interventionists all the time, but there might be situations where we actually find someone up for a dream of theirs, is that you can no longer make excuses about the situation when you're being hooked up with a dream. So this was his, this was the restaurant that John idolized, and so by getting a job there, he had to rise to the occasion. And then, of course, when he has been telling... So what's interesting is that first, at first he didn't, at first he felt right back into his sympathy and his story. Yes. So that's when Tony used the next strategy, which is the community meeting where he got people who John respects and whose opinions he values and needs to help him to rise to the occasion. Right. And so by the correct leverage of love and being who he could be for his children. So you know, I haven't seen... What was also a real theme also, Mark, in this intervention was that the focus on self versus the focus on the outer world, so that John's problem really on a deep level was that all of his focus was on his internal needs. He had no... he had lost all ability to see his children's needs or anyone around him. And so when pushed under the stressful environment of the restaurant, he again just focused on his own needs, not the needs of the restaurant or the opportunity or anyone else. Absolutely. And I think when people... there's a kind of reciprocal effect when people get really attached or, you know, I don't really like the metaphor of addicted because it tends to get overused. We definitely got, you know, disproportionately attached to short-term comforts, coping, self-soothing kind of, you know, sleeping, things like that, is that when we do that, we tend to shift our whole... We position socially and in ourselves to make that okay and make that the main thing. We tell ourselves, we deserve this, other people haven't been through the pain that we've been through, and we focus on meeting our needs and we, you know, can blank out the needs of other people or the experience of other people. Now, when we talk about the short-term, long-term, you know, this was something that Tony observed with John. And it's a... I would say that's probably the objective reality that he was using as his basis when helping the intervention, but he never went into speaking like this with John. He was always about finding... using a strategy to help him to step up and then any time, any little bit of progress was made, for instance, when he said something that was, you know, worthy of respect or that was a step in the direction of acknowledging, you know, other people's pain, acknowledging what other people needed, then he would get massive reinforcement from Tony. Yeah, so those are the... I think those are the main strategies that I saw here. And so I'll repeat them here. We have a slide, of course, but the jackpot strategy, again, is what disrupts the... When people are in this phase of being in the short-term and withholding and not giving to others and they're focusing on their own needs, they can become stubborn and they can become like you trying to lure them out to do something for other people, and they feel like it's a tit for tat or some kind of negotiation. So the jackpot strategy of taking them to Fiji, showering them with all sorts of amazing experiences was there to jolt them and to disrupt that kind of... You know, we can get... When we get selfish and we get all about ourselves to disrupt that selfishness. The second was correcting the hierarchy. So the father was taking care of the son and not the son taking care of the father and using that as a precedent for them, all the other... For the rest of the intervention. Third being the ordeal, again, putting it... It's creating a situation where John had to rise to the occasion and he had agreed to do it and he was with people who were not going to, you know, accept the typical appeals for, you know, come on, give me a break, let me have the hook. Reversal of privilege is when you feel like you're the most person, is the most justified or needy of other people's sympathy and then you realize that there's someone next to you who would have deserved much more sympathy than you, but they're actually have higher standards than you do. And then, of course, getting the job and getting the support of community. So the jackpot strategy is always to be used with benefits and positive observations stacking up of what one does have or what one has had in the past when you're talking to the client. Obviously, you're probably not going to fly them onto a vacation. Yeah. So it's not to load them up with negatives ever. The correcting the hierarchy, when you do this in a family, for example, you're still allowing the child to feel good about themselves. It's not a correction to bring one person down and one person up. It's that both people are important, but now the one who should really have responsibility steps up and takes responsibility. So you might ask the child, what could you do that's carefree or fun or just for you? And you'd ask the parent how to help the child have that experience. Reversal of privilege. It's not about shaming or saying, listen to this person who has it so much worse. Instead, it's having the experience of being with someone else who is living the example of having had something terrible happen to them like losing the leg and still getting up and doing what they wanted to do. In this case, it was extreme because he kept doing things for others at such a high level. But I think sometimes there's a tendency in coaching to use examples of items that are almost bragging or can end up shaming the client. And we never want to do that. We want to tell stories or give examples which will reverse the privilege, but not in a way that leads to shaming or negative feelings or the client disliking themselves further. I remember one was a little girl that was abused in an alcoholic family, molested. All kinds of terrible things in her childhood and we said she was going to become probably a prostitute and a heroin addict, most of the predictions. She was Eleanor Roosevelt. And Charlie Chaplin was raised in an orphanage because his mother was schizophrenic and he was very abused also. It was Charlie Chaplin.
